title: A. Paññāvaddho: "If you focus outwardly to contemplate something external – a person, an animal, a situation, a dead body, something loathsome – you first…
content:
Internal and External Body Contemplation

"If you focus outwardly to contemplate something external – a person, an animal, a situation, a dead body, something loathsome – you first think of it externally. Then you apply that to yourself by seeing the similarity between that and your own body. That brings it home. If you're focusing inwardly in the body contemplation, you focus on the internal body parts like the bones, the kidney, the heart, the liver, the lungs and so on. All these are internal objects that you investigate by focusing inward. When one deals with people and doesn’t want to break the meditation… Mindfulness is the thing that is important there. If you are mindful, you won’t forget yourself. Not forgetting yourself means that you talk to the person from inside yourself, from your own position. You don’t forget yourself and put all your attention out there.",

title: A. Paññāvaddho: That is actually true, but not in the way people normally think. It hinges on what is meant by “existence.” Change is the…
content:
Distinguishing Changing Existence from Unchanging Reality

That is actually true, but not in the way people normally think. It hinges on what is meant by “existence.” Change is the essence of existence and everything in the five khandhas is constantly changing; therefore the five khandhas are the domain of existence. Without change, there is no existence. On the other hand, changing phenomena are not real, because reality never changes. Thus the five khandhas exist, but they are not real. They are not reality because reality is not impermanent. Because it never changes, reality is not known through sensation; and because we can’t sense reality, it’s not associated with the five khandhas. However, reality can be known: We know it by being it.

title: A. Paññāvaddho: "This is the true meaning of the much-talked-about, and much misunderstood, Middle Way. Most people think that the Middle Way is like any…
content:
The True Meaning of the Middle Way as Balancing Against Kilesas

"This is the true meaning of the much-talked-about, and much misunderstood, Middle Way. Most people think that the Middle Way is like any good old English compromise. But that isn't true. The Middle Way indicates that path that is necessary to counter the kilesas. Because of the nature of their kilesas, some people have to practice the strictest austerities to counter their kilesas. For such people, those strict austerities are the Middle Way. Other people, because their kilesas are weaker, and practice with more ease and comfort. For them, that will be the Middle Way. When you go in the direction of the kilesas, you are moving away from the center. In some cases, practicing unnecessary austerities is actually going in the direction of the kilesas, and therefore throwing your practice out of the proper balance. Countering the kilesas means bringing your mind and your practice back to the center; that is, back into balance. The Middle Way is a matter of balancing the practice against the kilesas. The Middle Way really means a way of developing dispassion."

title: A. Paññāvaddho: "In the end, the highest choice we can make is to choose the path to enlightenment, which focuses our attention towards following the…
content:
Choosing the Path to Enlightenment

"In the end, the highest choice we can make is to choose the path to enlightenment, which focuses our attention towards following the Noble Eightfold Path to a clear understanding of the Four Noble Truths. This is the choice that leads to indisputable happiness. All other choices should be evaluated in light of whether they support or obstruct this fundamental intention. This is what the Lord Buddha meant when he spoke of yoniso manasikara, or wise reflection. Through such reflection, we can use our ability to choose skillfully, transforming it from a source of confusion and disappointment into a tool for seeking the liberating truths of the Buddha's teaching. So, what is the root cause of our suffering? Look carefully, and you will see it stems from the mind's habit of grasping and clinging. The mind moves restlessly from one desire to another, always craving, always thirsting for something new. And what helps to fuel this endless wanting?"

title: A. Paññāvaddho: "Ottappa has an external focus and arises out of respect for others. Both imply an awareness of the suffering that will result if…
content:
Distinguishing Internal and External Moral Focus

"Ottappa has an external focus and arises out of respect for others. Both imply an awareness of the suffering that will result if we engage in immoral conduct. These two qualities work together but from different angles. Think of it like this: when you're about to do something immoral, hiri is that voice inside that says, 'I'm better than this; acting so poorly is beneath my dignity,' whereas ottappa is the voice that says, 'What about how my behavior will affect others? Do you really want to suffer the consequences of this improper conduct?'. The difference between hiri and ottappa can be illustrated using the simile of an iron rod where one end of the rod is smeared with feces and the other end is heated until it is red-hot. A wise person would neither grasp the filthy end out of a feeling of disgust nor grasp the red-hot end for fear of being burned."

title: A. Paññāvaddho: "Through his supreme enlightenment, the Buddha was able to show us not just the nature of existence, but also the path to liberation…
content:
The Narrow Windows of Scientific and Theological Views

"Through his supreme enlightenment, the Buddha was able to show us not just the nature of existence, but also the path to liberation from its endless cycle... This is the heart of the Buddha's teaching: that through developing virtue and wisdom, any being can follow in his footsteps and attain to this same liberation. When we speak of the Lord Buddha's enlightenment, we must understand that it was a total opening of mind. Not just a partial understanding, but a complete penetration of reality. In that state, "kamma" and rebirth were seen directly, not as theories or beliefs, but as fundamental truths of existence. Unfortunately, most of us are like people living in a very small room with tiny windows. We peer out through these windows and think we see the whole world, but we're seeing only fragments of the full picture. The scientists look through one window and see only material phenomena. The theologians look through another and see only their creator God.",

title: A. Paññāvaddho: "Because we always want an identity and, to establish an identity, we’ve got to establish what is not us. What is not our…
content:
The Promotion of Self through Identity

"Because we always want an identity and, to establish an identity, we’ve got to establish what is not us. What is not our self. And, because of that, we establish the world, the whole world, and everybody, other people – everything: 'That’s not my self, but it defines where “I” am. I am in the middle of that, so to speak.' And these thoughts, in all sorts of ways, are trying to establish what one is. Memory helps in this too, because one has memories from the past and that establishes what one is, based on the past. And these memories keep coming up, and one keeps thinking about them and keeps refreshing them so that one can be quite sure of one’s environment. Memory is established there all of the time, and the constant bringing up of thoughts of the past – from all over the place – which one has thought about before many, many times: one does this just to confirm one’s situation – to confirm one’s environment – to confirm that 'This is ME here'."

title: A. Paññāvaddho: "As the middle watch approached, his mind's eye opened still further to encompass all living beings throughout the cosmos. He witnessed directly how…
content:
The Cosmic Law of Kamma as Direct Knowledge

"As the middle watch approached, his mind's eye opened still further to encompass all living beings throughout the cosmos. He witnessed directly how beings arise and pass away, moving from one state to another in accordance with their "kamma". Like actors in an infinite drama, all beings are bound together in an intricate web of relationships extending throughout time, their destinies shaped by the inexorable law of cause and effect and determined by the quality of actions and their consequences. The supreme enlightenment that dawned in the Buddha's consciousness was not mere philosophical speculation or theoretical inference... On the contrary, it was direct knowledge born of perfect understanding—an immediate and unmediated perception of the cosmic law of "kamma" working itself out through the lives of countless beings. The Buddha perceived with absolute clarity how each action—whether of body, speech, or mind—sets in motion ripples of future consequences that must inevitably bear fruit.",

title: A. Paññāvaddho: "With constant practice of body contemplation we come finally to realize that the body is completely separate. It is not me, and never…
content:
The Components of Mind and the "One Who Knows"

"With constant practice of body contemplation we come finally to realize that the body is completely separate. It is not me, and never was me. It has always belonged to the world. This is a very profound state of realization. We then turn our full attention to the mind and ask the question: what is the mind?. The mind is a mixture of four aspects: feeling, memory, thought and consciousness. All four of them come from one essential nature we call the citta. The citta is “the one who knows”, the underlying basis of all knowing within us. It’s central to every form of consciousness, and it’s always there. The one who knows has been trapped by the kilesas into tuning itself into consciousness, into thought, into feeling and into memory. Because of that, the one who knows is not free and so is always causing us trouble. It causes us trouble because it’s been infiltrated by the defilements within our own hearts. The Dhamma, the pure way, is the original nature of the one who knows, and it also resides within the heart."

title: A. Paññāvaddho: Craving operates as a system that permeates every aspect of our consciousness and shapes how we engage with reality itself. This force perpetually…
content:
Craving in the Subliminal Levels of Sleep

Craving operates as a system that permeates every aspect of our consciousness and shapes how we engage with reality itself. This force perpetually conditions our emotional states, coloring every perception that arises in awareness. To illustrate how this all-pervasive craving never ceases: even when we're sleeping, craving continues to flow at the subliminal level of sleep consciousness. It affects the quality of our sleep, the content of our dreams, and the mood we wake up in. Craving's underlying dissatisfaction sets the tone for every conscious moment.
